factual-accuracy.requirement: States independent director is person who is not executive officer/employee and has no relationship interfering with independent judgment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response defines an independent director as someone with no material relationship that could interfere with independent judgment and states they are not independent if they or an immediate family member were an executive officer or employee within the last three years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States board must make affirmative determination of each director's independence based on all relevant facts
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that independence requires an “affirmative subjective determination by the board” that assesses “all relevant facts and circumstances” and affirms no material relationship exists. This directly matches the requirement that the board must make an affirmative independence determination based on all relevant facts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Defines Family Member as spouse, parents, children, siblings, in-laws (mothers/fathers/sons/daughters/brothers/sisters-in-law), and anyone sharing director''s home (excluding domestic employees)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly defines “immediate family members” to include spouse/domestic partner, parents, children, siblings, the specified in-laws (mother/father/son/daughter/brother/sister-in-law), and anyone residing in the director’s household other than domestic employees.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director cannot be currently employed by company or have been employed within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a director is not independent if they or an immediate family member have been employed by the company or its subsidiaries within the last three years, which implies they also cannot be currently employed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director's Family Member cannot be or have been executive officer of company within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a director is not independent if an immediate family member is currently, or was within the past three years, an executive officer of the company or its subsidiaries (see the “Employment Relationship” and “Familial Relationships” sections).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States $120,000 direct compensation threshold for any 12-month period within last 3 years disqualifies independence
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that receiving more than $120,000 in direct compensation during any consecutive 12-month period within the last three years disqualifies a director from being independent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States board/committee fees, pension benefits under tax-qualified plans, and non-discretionary compensation excluded from $120K threshold
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that compensation excluded from the $120,000 threshold includes board/committee fees and “pension or other forms of deferred compensation not contingent on continued service,” and it also mentions payments under broad-based plans like 401(k) matching (tax-qualified plans).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States business relationship disqualification threshold is greater of 5% of recipient company's consolidated gross revenues OR $200,000
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that business dealings exceeding the greater of $200,000 or 5% of the recipient’s annual gross revenues within the last three fiscal years presumptively negate independence. This matches the required disqualification threshold formulation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States business relationship threshold applies to payments in current or any of past 3 fiscal years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says business dealings/payments exceeding the threshold disqualify independence if they occur “within the last three fiscal years” and “for any fiscal year within that period,” which covers the current fiscal year and any of the past three fiscal years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot be current partner of company's outside auditor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states under “Auditor Affiliation” that directors or immediate family members who are current partners of the company’s independent registered public accounting firm are disqualified, which covers a director or family member being a current partner of the outside auditor.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot have been partner/employee of outside auditor who worked on company's audit within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that directors or immediate family members who are current partners/principals/employees of the company’s independent accounting firm, or who were so within the last three years, are disqualified. This covers the required rule that a director or family member cannot have been a partner/employee of the outside auditor within the past 3 years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot be/have been executive officer of entity where company's executives serve on that entity's compensation committee (current or within past 3 years)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses cross-company compensation committee conflicts only in terms of directors serving on compensation committees, but it does not state the specific NASDAQ bright-line disqualification that the director or a family member cannot be/have been an executive officer of an entity where the company’s executives serve on that entity’s compensation committee within the past three years. The required executive-officer/family-member and 3-year lookback element is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States for investment companies under Investment Company Act of 1940, interested person (other than solely due to board service) is not independent
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses registered investment companies being exempt from NASDAQ independence rules, but it does not state that under the Investment Company Act of 1940 an “interested person” (other than solely due to board service) is not independent. The specific ICA 1940 “interested person” independence disqualification is not addressed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States majority independent board required for listed companies, subject to Rule 5615 exemptions (controlled companies, investment companies, asset-backed issuers)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that regular Nasdaq-listed companies must have a majority independent board and separately describes Rule 5615 exemptions for controlled companies and registered investment companies. However, it does not mention Rule 5615 exemptions for asset-backed issuers, so the full set of specified exemptions is not covered.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States audit, compensation, and nominating committees must be composed entirely of independent directors (with limited exemptions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the audit, compensation, and nominating/governance committees must be composed entirely of independent directors, and it notes limited exceptions such as cure periods, transitional arrangements, and an “exceptional and limited circumstances” exemption for compensation committees.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Defines controlled company as one where more than 50% of voting power held by individual, group, or another company
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly defines a controlled company as one where an individual, group, or other company owns more than 50% of voting power, matching the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States controlled companies exempt from majority independent board and independent compensation/nominating committee requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a “Controlled Companies” section stating they are exempt from the majority independent director requirement and from independent compensation and nominating committee composition requirements.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States controlled companies still must maintain audit committee meeting SEC Rule 10A-3 independence requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says controlled companies are “not exempt from” independent audit committee requirements and repeatedly notes audit committee independence must comply with SEC Rule 10A-3. However, it does not explicitly state that controlled companies still must maintain audit committee meeting SEC Rule 10A-3 independence requirements as a specific point.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States upon ceasing controlled status, majority of independent committee members required within 90 days
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses controlled companies losing controlled status but states compliance must be achieved “within one year/12 months,” not that a majority of independent committee members is required within 90 days. It does not mention any 90-day requirement tied to ceasing controlled status. The specific “majority of independent committee members within 90 days” element is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States all independent committee members required within one year and majority independent board within twelve months of ceasing controlled status
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that when a controlled company ceases controlled status it must achieve full compliance “within one year/12 months,” including majority independent board and independent committees. However, it does not explicitly state that all independent committee members are required within one year while the majority independent board is required within twelve months as separate requirements.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States foreign private issuers may follow home country practices but must have audit committee meeting NASDAQ and SEC requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: In the “Foreign Private Issuers (FPIs)” section, the response says FPIs may follow home country governance practices but must still comply with SEC Rule 10A-3 audit committee independence, describing an SEC/NASDAQ-compliant independent audit committee requirement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States audit committee members are prohibited from accepting consulting, advisory, or other compensatory fees beyond board/committee fees
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that audit committee members “cannot receive consulting or advisory fees aside from board/committee fees” and are “prohibited from receiving any compensation…other than director fees,” which covers a prohibition on consulting, advisory, or other compensatory fees beyond board/committee fees.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States stock ownership alone does not preclude independence
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that “the ownership of company stock alone, even if significant, does not impair independence,” indicating stock ownership alone does not preclude independence.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Presents NYSE independence thresholds or rules as NASDAQ requirements without distinguishing between the two exchanges
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes independence standards as NASDAQ Rule 5605 requirements and does not label NYSE thresholds as NASDAQ rules. While it includes a $200,000 or 5% gross revenues business-relationship test that resembles NYSE-style bright-line criteria, it is not explicitly presented as an NYSE rule being applied to NASDAQ without distinction.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: For at least 3 exemption types, states both what requirements are exempt AND what requirements still apply
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response covers at least three exemption types—controlled companies, IPO phase-ins, and foreign private issuers—and for each it states what is exempt (e.g., majority-independent board/committee composition) and what still applies (e.g., independent audit committee under SEC Rule 10A-3 and/or disclosure obligations).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes BDCs must comply with Rule 5600 Series as they are not exempt like registered management investment companies under 1940 Act
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly distinguishes registered investment companies as generally exempt from NASDAQ Rule 5605 independence requirements, while stating that Business Development Companies must comply fully with NASDAQ independence and committee composition rules (citing the 5600 Series).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly distinguishes compensation test exclusions (board fees, pension, non-discretionary) from business relationship exclusions (securities investments, charitable matching)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response separately describes compensation threshold exclusions (e.g., board/committee fees, pension/deferred compensation, broad-based plan matching) and then, in a distinct section on business relationships, describes disqualifying business dealings/financial transactions. However, it does not explicitly identify business-relationship exclusions as “securities investments” or “charitable matching.”

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States all 3 controlled company phase-in timelines (immediate requirement, 90-day requirement, one-year requirement)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions an immediate requirement (SEC Rule 10A-3 audit committee independence) and a one-year/12-month requirement for controlled companies and IPO phase-ins, but it does not state a distinct 90-day controlled company phase-in timeline. The specific “90-day requirement” element is missing from the controlled company timelines.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly states audit committee has additional/enhanced independence tests beyond standard board-level criteria
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that “stricter standards apply to audit committee members” and describes layered audit committee independence requirements under NASDAQ Rule 5605(c) plus SEC Rule 10A-3, beyond general board independence criteria.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies at least 6 distinct exemption categories with substantive explanation for each
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response identifies at least six exemption/special-case categories—controlled companies, IPO phase-ins, foreign private issuers (home-country practice), registered investment companies, business development companies, and committee-level exceptions (e.g., compensation committee “exceptional and limited circumstances”)—and provides substantive explanations of what requirements are waived/modified and what still applies for each.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains three-year lookback runs from relationship end and notes brief interim executive service exception
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions a three-year lookback but says it runs from the date of board determination, not from the end of the relationship. It also does not note the NASDAQ exception that brief interim executive officer service (typically under one year) does not trigger the three-year employment lookback. Both required elements are missing/incorrect.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes binding obligations pre-dating November 2003 may exempt companies from independent nominating committee requirements
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses nominating committee independence requirements and exemptions for controlled companies, FPIs, and phase-ins, but it does not mention any exemption tied to binding obligations that pre-date November 2003. There is no reference to pre-2003 agreements affecting the independent nominating committee requirement. The specific November 2003 binding-obligation exemption is absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes between current auditor partner disqualification and former partner/employee who worked on company audit
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses auditor affiliation generally (current or within last three years) but does not distinguish between a current auditor partner being disqualified versus a former partner/employee who specifically worked on the company’s audit. It lacks the nuance about former audit engagement involvement versus mere prior employment. This specific distinction is not addressed.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Treats controlled companies, foreign private issuers, and investment companies as having identical exemption scopes without distinguishing their different retained requirements
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response distinguishes the exemption scopes for controlled companies, foreign private issuers, and registered investment companies by separately describing what each is exempt from and what requirements they still must meet (notably audit committee independence and disclosure obligations). It does not treat these categories as having identical exemptions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses formal legal terminology (executive officer, affiliated person, compensatory fees, disqualification criteria)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses formal legal terminology such as “executive officer,” “disqualified,” “affiliated entity,” and repeatedly discusses “disqualification” and “compensation” thresholds (i.e., compensatory fees/compensation concepts) as part of the independence analysis.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes content with clear sections for definition, disqualifications, eligibility, and company requirements
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into labeled sections covering the definition (Section I), disqualifications (Section II), eligibility criteria (also Section II and the thresholds summary), and company requirements (Section IV on requirements by company type). These headings clearly separate the requested content areas.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective regulatory tone appropriate for compliance documentation without advisory recommendations beyond rule requirements
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple advisory recommendations and practical guidance (e.g., “Implement detailed annual questionnaires,” “Monitor… closely,” “Foster clear communication”), which goes beyond stating rule requirements. This shifts the tone from objective compliance documentation into prescriptive advice, so it does not maintain the requested purely regulatory tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual expressions such as "basically", "get a pass", "who's in/out", contractions, or colloquial phrasing
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes casual/colloquial phrasing such as “get a pass” (in the FPI Section 16 discussion) and uses contractions like “who’s” in the criterion-relevant sense.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same disqualification criterion or exemption multiple times using different wording without adding new regulatory detail
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats the same exemptions and disqualification concepts multiple times (e.g., controlled company exemptions, IPO phase-ins, and audit committee independence/no phase-in) across several sections (IV, V, VI) with rewording but little new regulatory detail beyond the initial statements.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2) for independent director definition and disqualification criteria
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites “NASDAQ Rule 5605(a)(2)” as the source of the independent director definition and then lists multiple bright-line disqualification criteria (employment, compensation thresholds, business relationships, auditor affiliation, family relationships, etc.) tied to that rule.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(b)(1) for majority independent board requirement
citation-quality.explanation: The response references “NASDAQ Rule 5605(b)” and “Board Majority Independence” but never cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(b)(1) specifically. It does not include the “(b)(1)” subsection citation for the majority independent board requirement.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites SEC Rule 10A-3 for audit committee independence requirements
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites SEC Rule 10A-3 multiple times when discussing audit committee independence requirements (e.g., in the IPO companies section and the audit committee independence section).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5615 or IM-5605 for exemptions or interpretive guidance
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5615 multiple times when discussing exemptions and phase-in schedules (e.g., controlled companies under Rule 5615(c)(1) and IPO companies under Rule 5615(b)). It does not cite IM-5605, but Rule 5615 is explicitly referenced for exemptions.

citation-quality.requirement: Relies exclusively on legal blogs, law firm marketing materials, or Wikipedia without citing primary NASDAQ rules or SEC regulations
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites primary sources including the official Nasdaq Listing Center rulebook (Rule 5605) and SEC materials (e.g., SEC proposed rule change PDF, Federal Register notice, SEC final rule). Because it does not rely exclusively on legal blogs, law firm marketing materials, or Wikipedia, the problematic reliance described is absent.
